clean_story:
Value exchanged. Resources measured.

The word cost comes from the Latin constare, meaning "to stand firm"—because a price is what holds steady between what you give and what you receive. Like a seesaw balancing effort and reward.

Cost isn’t just money. It’s time spent waiting in line instead of playing. It’s the calories burned to hunt a rabbit versus gathering berries. A farmer trading three chickens for a plow calculates cost in eggs not laid, not just coins. Factories track it in machine wear: every 10,000 widgets shaves 0.2 millimeters off a drill bit, demanding replacement.

Hidden costs lurk. A "free" app might cost attention—ads flash 28 times per minute, fracturing focus. Cheap shoes cost more long-term if soles crack after 500 steps. Governments measure cost in lives: a highway’s speed limit balances fuel savings against crash fatalities (data shows 1 mile-per-hour increase raises deaths by 3–5%).

Three related subjects are trade-offs in nature, the psychology of spending, and how cities budget invisibly.
